questionText: Prove that $\arcsin x + \arccos x = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ for all $x \in [-1, 1]$.

steps.description: Set out what must be proved
steps.explanation: The plan is to prove it first for $x\in[0,1]$ using a right triangle whose two acute angles are $\arcsin x$ and $\arccos x$, then extend to negative $x$ using the reflection properties of the inverse functions.

steps.description: Introduce the angle for non-negative x
steps.explanation: For non-negative $x$ the inverse sine gives an angle in the first quadrant, which can be drawn as an acute angle in a right triangle.
Choosing hypotenuse $1$ makes $\sin\alpha=\dfrac{\text{opposite}}{1}=x$ automatically, so the side opposite $\alpha$ has length $x$.
Pythagoras gives the remaining side; it is real because $x\in[0,1]$.
In any right triangle the two non-right angles add to $\tfrac{\pi}{2}$, because the three angles sum to $\pi$ and one of them is the right angle. Call the second one $\gamma$.
The side opposite $\alpha$ is the side adjacent to $\gamma$, so $\cos\gamma=\dfrac{x}{1}=x$. This is the bridge to the inverse cosine.
Since $\gamma$ lies inside the range $[0,\pi]$ of $\arccos$ and has cosine $x$, it must be exactly $\arccos x$.
Adding the two acute angles, the $\alpha$ terms cancel and the sum is $\tfrac\pi2$, proving the identity for $x\in[0,1]$.
The boundary values behave correctly, confirming the triangle argument covers the whole interval $[0,1]$.

steps.description: Prepare to extend to negative x
steps.workingLatex: \text{Now take } x \in [0,1] \text{ and consider the argument } -x
steps.explanation: To reach negative inputs we use the known symmetry (reflection) properties of the two inverse functions, which are standard graph features.

steps.description: Use the odd symmetry of arcsin
steps.explanation: The graph of $\arcsin$ has rotational symmetry about the origin, so it is an odd function.

steps.description: Use the reflection property of arccos
steps.explanation: The graph of $\arccos$ is symmetric about the point $\big(0,\tfrac\pi2\big)$, giving this reflection relationship.

steps.description: Add the two for the argument minus x
steps.explanation: Substituting the two symmetry results sets up a sum that can be simplified using the positive-$x$ case already proved.
Since $\arcsin x+\arccos x=\tfrac\pi2$ for $x\in[0,1]$, the bracket is $\tfrac\pi2$, and the sum for negative arguments is also $\tfrac\pi2$.
The identity holds for non-negative $x$ by the triangle argument and for negative $x$ by symmetry, so it is true across the entire domain.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: For $x\in[0,1]$ the two acute angles of a right triangle with hypotenuse $1$ are $\arcsin x$ and $\arccos x$, which sum to $\tfrac\pi2$; the odd symmetry of $\arcsin$ and $\arccos(-x)=\pi-\arccos x$ extend this to $[-1,1]$. Hence $\arcsin x+\arccos x=\tfrac\pi2$.
